label: Multisig and key separation
brief:
A k-of-n multisig wallet requires k signatures from a set of n keys to spend. The most common configurations are 2-of-3 (one key for daily access, one offsite backup, one with a third party or in a safe deposit box) and 3-of-5 for larger amounts [1]. The point is to make both theft and loss require compromising more than one location: stealing one key is useless without a second, losing one key still leaves you with k-of-(n-1) recovery options.

What actually matters in a multisig design is what the keys don't share. Keys held on the same kind of device, in the same room, by the same person, with the same backup method are correlated failure points; an attacker who learns to compromise one learns to compromise them all. Vendor diversity (different signer manufacturers), geographic diversity (keys in different physical locations), and seed-derivation diversity (don't reuse the same mnemonic across keys) all matter — the goal is for any single failure mode to take out at most n-k keys [2]. A 2-of-3 where all three keys live on identical signers in the same drawer is, for security purposes, a 1-of-1 with extra steps.

The operational tax is real and worth pricing in honestly. Recovering a multisig requires not just the keys but the wallet's descriptor (the script template that says which keys, in what order, with what derivation paths) — losing the descriptor while keeping the keys is a recoverable but painful situation, and losing both is unrecoverable. Inheritance becomes a multi-stakeholder coordination problem. Routine spending requires touching multiple devices. Most users with non-trivial holdings end up at 2-of-3, with the trade-off that complexity is bounded and the legal/operational story is comprehensible to a non-technical heir or co-signer [3]. Going beyond that is justifiable but not free.

sources.label: Bitcoin Wiki — Multisignature
sources.excerpt: Multisignature transactions require multiple private keys to sign. k-of-n schemes are the most common, with 2-of-3 being the standard self-custody configuration. The security gain is that an attacker must compromise k of the n keys; the resilience gain is that the user can lose up to n-k keys without losing access to funds.

sources.label: Bitcoin Optech — Topic: Multisignature
sources.excerpt: Effective multisig depends on key separation that is real along every relevant axis: device, location, custodian, derivation. Configurations that share a manufacturer, a physical location, or an underlying entropy source provide weaker security than the k-of-n math suggests because a single failure mode can take out multiple keys simultaneously.

sources.label: Glacier Protocol — Self-custody for high-value Bitcoin
sources.excerpt: Glacier specifies a multisig cold-storage protocol for protecting large Bitcoin holdings against both theft and accidental loss. The design covers entropy generation, signer selection, geographic distribution, and recovery rehearsal. The full document is over 100 pages — the operational complexity of a high-assurance multisig setup is real and should be acknowledged before adopting it.
